skill.name: peer-review-response-drafter
skill.description: Drafts professional, point-by-point response letters to peer reviewer comments for academic journal submissions. Supports multiple tone styles and input formats.
skill.summary: Generate structured, polite, and effective response letters to peer reviewer comments for academic manuscripts.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 112 potential issues across 6 files including shell execution patterns, weak cryptography references, and heuristic combinations. After AI evaluation, all high-severity and critical findings were determined to be false positives: backtick marks are markdown code block syntax (not shell execution), cryptography keywords appear in template text and documentation (no crypto code exists), system reconnaissance patterns are spurious matches on common words, and path traversal flags hit security checklist items that document protections. The only confirmed findings are legitimate file I/O operations for writing output response letters and a static documentation URL. The skill processes text entirely locally with no external API calls, no credential access, and no code injection vectors.

low_findings.title: File write operations to user-controlled paths
low_findings.description: The Python script writes generated response letters to file paths provided by the user via command-line arguments (--output) and interactive input. While legitimate for a document generation tool, paths are not validated against directory traversal sequences, allowing writes outside the intended workspace if the user provides paths containing '../'.

low_findings.title: Static URL reference in documentation
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded GitHub URL pointing to the skill's source repository. This is a reference/documentation link, not a runtime network call. The Python script makes no network requests and processes all data locally.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown code blocks misidentified as shell execution
dangerous_patterns.description: All 67 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown code fence delimiters (```) used for formatting code examples and templates in documentation files (SKILL.md, response_templates.md). No actual shell execution, subprocess calls, or command injection vectors exist. These are false positives from pattern matching on backtick characters.

dangerous_patterns.title: Template text misidentified as weak cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: All 28 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are false positives. Words like 'key' (in .keys() method calls), 'des' (in 'describe'), and hash references (documentation metadata) trigger pattern matches. No cryptographic operations exist in any file. The Python script performs only text processing with the standard library.

content.user_title: Draft Professional Peer Review Response Letters
content.value_statement: Responding to peer reviewer comments is stressful and time-consuming for academic researchers. This skill drafts professional, point-by-point response letters that maintain a respectful tone while clearly addressing each reviewer concern.
actual_capabilities: Parse reviewer comments from multiple input formats including plain text, structured JSON, and markdown
Generate point-by-point response letters with proper salutation, numbered responses, and professional closing
Adjust response tone between diplomatic, formal, and assertive styles to match journal expectations
Match responses to numbered reviewer comments for clear one-to-one correspondence
Include manuscript location references such as page and line numbers in each response
Run as an interactive wizard for guided setup or as an automated command-line tool for batch processing
limitations: Does not verify factual accuracy of response content - human review of all generated text is required
Cannot access or read your actual manuscript files - you must provide all change descriptions and locations
Generated responses use template-based language that may need customization for your specific academic field
Requires Python 3.10 or later to run the packaged script workflow

use_cases.title: Junior Researcher Preparing a First Revision
use_cases.description: An early-career researcher needs help drafting professional responses to reviewer comments for their first journal submission. The skill provides structured templates and appropriate academic language to build confidence.
use_cases.target_user: Early-career academic researchers and graduate students

use_cases.title: Experienced Author Handling Difficult Reviews
use_cases.description: A senior researcher faces reviewer comments that require careful diplomatic handling. The skill offers tone-adjusted templates to maintain professionalism while defending methodological choices with evidence-based rationales.

use_cases.title: Research Lab Coordinating Multi-Author Revisions
use_cases.description: A corresponding author coordinates revisions across multiple co-authors. The skill generates consistent response formatting and ensures every reviewer comment across multiple reviewers receives a corresponding numbered reply.
use_cases.target_user: Research lab managers and corresponding authors

prompt_templates.title: Basic Response Draft
prompt_templates.prompt:
Help me draft a response to these reviewer comments for my manuscript titled "[Title]" submitted to [Journal Name]. Here are the reviewer comments:

[paste reviewer comments here]

I have made the following changes to the manuscript:
[list your changes with locations]
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user with straightforward revision requests and clear changes

prompt_templates.title: Tone-Adjusted Response for Sensitive Comments
prompt_templates.prompt:
Draft a response letter to the following reviewer comments using a [diplomatic/formal/assertive] tone. Manuscript title: "[Title]" for [Journal Name]. 

Reviewer comments:
[paste comments]

My changes and rationale for each comment:
[list changes with brief justifications for any disagreements]
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a specific tone to handle sensitive or critical reviewer feedback

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Reviewer Response with Conflicting Feedback
prompt_templates.prompt:
I received reviewer comments from two reviewers who disagree with each other. Reviewer 1 suggests [approach A], but Reviewer 2 suggests [approach B]. Help me draft a response that acknowledges both perspectives respectfully and explains my chosen approach. Manuscript title: "[Title]" for [Journal Name]. Here are all the comments:

[paste all reviewer comments]
prompt_templates.scenario: Handling conflicting reviewer suggestions while maintaining a diplomatic tone

prompt_templates.title: Interactive Script Workflow with Full Customization
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the interactive peer review response drafter for my manuscript. I need to: 1) Enter manuscript title, journal name, and author list. 2) Paste all reviewer comments. 3) Describe my changes for each individual comment including page and line numbers. 4) Select diplomatic tone. 5) Generate the complete response letter and save it as a markdown file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user running the Python script in interactive mode for full control over each response

output_examples.input:
Reviewer 1:
1. The introduction should better motivate the problem.
2. Figure 2 is unclear.

My changes: Added motivation paragraph to introduction. Redrew Figure 2 with clearer labels.
output_examples.output:
Dear Editor and Reviewers,

Thank you for your constructive feedback on our manuscript. We have carefully addressed all comments and revised the manuscript accordingly.

## Reviewer #1

**Comment 1:** The introduction should better motivate the problem.

**Response:** We thank the reviewer for this valuable suggestion. We have revised the introduction to include a stronger motivation paragraph that highlights the gap in current knowledge and the significance of addressing it (Page 2, Lines 15-30).

**Comment 2:** Figure 2 is unclear.

**Response:** We appreciate the reviewer's insightful comment. We have redrawn Figure 2 with clearer axis labels, improved color contrast, and a more descriptive caption to ensure readability (Page 5, Figure 2).

We hope these revisions satisfactorily address all concerns.

Sincerely,
[Author Names]

output_examples.input:
Reviewer 1:
1. Have you considered using method X instead of method Y for your primary analysis?

My response: I want to keep method Y because the data is non-normal and method Y handles this better. I added a justification in the methods section.
output_examples.output:
## Reviewer #1

**Comment 1:** Have you considered using method X instead of method Y for your primary analysis?

**Response:** We thank the reviewer for this thoughtful suggestion. After careful evaluation, we have maintained our current approach using method Y. Our reasoning is that method Y is more appropriate for the specific data characteristics in our study, particularly the non-normal distribution of our primary outcome variable. Method X assumes normality and would require transformations that could complicate interpretation. We have added a detailed justification for this methodological choice in the Methods section (Page 6, Lines 80-95).

best_practices: Always review generated responses for factual accuracy and scientific correctness before submitting to any journal
Provide clear descriptions of your changes with specific page and line number references for each reviewer comment
Use diplomatic tone as the default and switch to formal or assertive only when you have a specific reason to do so
anti_patterns: Do not submit generated responses without thorough human review - the skill cannot verify scientific accuracy, check citations, or validate your methodology
Avoid using assertive tone for all responses - it can appear confrontational to editors and reviewers when a diplomatic approach would be more appropriate
Do not skip numbering your responses or leave any reviewer comment unaddressed - every comment must have a corresponding numbered reply in the final letter

faq.question: What input formats does this skill accept for reviewer comments?
faq.answer: The skill accepts copy-pasted reviewer comments as plain text, PDF extracted text, structured JSON with comment IDs, and markdown with sections. You can also use the interactive mode to enter comments directly during a guided session.

faq.question: Can this skill verify the scientific accuracy of my responses?
faq.answer: No. The skill helps draft professional language and structures your responses logically, but it cannot verify scientific accuracy, check citations, or validate your methodology. You must carefully review all generated content before submitting to a journal.

faq.question: Does this skill send my manuscript content or reviewer comments to external servers?
faq.answer: No. All processing happens locally on your machine. The Python script runs entirely offline without making any external API calls or network requests. Your reviewer comments, manuscript details, and generated responses remain private.

faq.question: What tone options are available for response letters?
faq.answer: Three tone styles are available. Diplomatic is warm and collaborative, recommended for most situations. Formal is detached and scholarly, well-suited for high-impact journals. Assertive is direct and confident, useful when defending against incorrect criticisms. Each tone adjusts vocabulary and phrasing accordingly.

faq.question: Can this skill handle reviewer comments in languages other than English?
faq.answer: The skill is designed for English-language academic correspondence. While it can process reviewer comments written in other languages, it generates responses in English using standard academic phrasing conventions common in international scientific publishing.

faq.question: How do I run the Python script that comes with this skill?
faq.answer: Install Python 3.10 or later, then run 'pip install -r requirements.txt' to set up dependencies. Use 'python scripts/main.py --interactive' for the guided setup wizard, or 'python scripts/main.py --input-file comments.txt --title "Your Title" --journal "Journal Name"' for automated command-line use.
